{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks which marker will be increased in a patient with Hepatitis B infection. From the provided documents, we learn that HBsAg (Hepatitis B surface antigen) is the first virological marker to be detected thanks to its exposure on the viral surface and is indicative of an acute infection. The presence of HBsAg rarely persists beyond 6 months after infection and typically precedes detectable quantities of the corresponding antibody to surface antigen (Anti-HBsAg). Other markers like HbcAg (Hepatitis B core antigen) and Anti-HbcAg IgG (antibody to Hepatitis B core antigen IgG) are not primary indicators of acute infection and are more associated with past exposure or chronic infection. Anti-HBsAg IgG (antibody to Hepatitis B surface antigen IgG) appears after recovery from acute infection or from vaccination, not during an acute infection.", "answer_choice": "A"}
